episode_title: Garance Burke: A Journalist's Guide to Investigating Artificial Intelligence

theme_codings.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: Human-AI Labor Division in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: At AP, we're really very cautious about implementing any AI tools. Our journalism always starts with a human being and ends with a human being.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke describes AP's strict human-starts-human-ends principle for AI use, mentions transcription as the primary proven productivity win, and emphasizes that journalists don't need engineering backgrounds to investigate AI systems, establishing the irreplaceable human role in both using and scrutinizing AI.

theme_codings.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: I think that taking it back to those journalistic basics of how the systems work, where they're deployed, how well these models are performing, whether the tools are regulated, who's benefiting and making money as a result of AI's growth and then which communities may be impacted by these tools, whether that be negatively or positively, can help anchor a lot of the journalism around AI so that we get out of this kind of tiresome binary, frankly, of is AI good or bad.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke frames the trust crisis through concrete misinformation examples -- chatbots telling Philadelphia residents there's no polling place and California voters they can vote by text -- while arguing that journalism must transcend the good/bad binary to help the public understand how AI actually works and who it affects.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles to Practice

best_quote.text: This is not a beat in which you want to be taking AI companies press releases as the final word.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke provides a journalistic ethics framework for AI coverage centered on who/what/where/when/why, advocates for investigating training data composition and community impacts, and warns against press-release journalism on AI, positioning AP Stylebook's AI chapter as an operational ethics guide for the profession.

theme_codings.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Choices

best_quote.text: Sometimes these large language models are so complex that even the engineers who built them can't exactly explain what's happening under the hood.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke addresses the opacity of AI systems as both a technical reality and an investigative challenge, noting that even engineers cannot explain LLM behaviors, and advocating for multiple methodologies (GitHub logs, synthetic datasets, community interviews) when code access is unavailable.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling, Adoption, and the Knowledge Gap

best_quote.text: You really don't need to be an engineer to interrogate these systems or understand them. I think that really focusing on the people who build AI tools, who are affected by AI tools... there's always human beings who you can access.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke democratizes AI literacy by arguing journalists already have the 'street smarts' to understand AI, countering the perception that statistical complexity places AI reporting beyond average reporters' reach, and positioning the AP Stylebook AI chapter as a literacy resource.

theme_codings.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Change

best_quote.text: I think it's important to think about both the upside, but also proceed cautiously. There's an opportunity to have deeper ethical discussions about what is the right way to implement AI tooling in our sacred news production.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke briefly touches on organizational strategy, noting AP's cautious approach to AI implementation and the need for newsrooms to have deeper ethical discussions about when AI assistance is appropriate in news production.

theme_codings.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: AI companies are seeking to extend their power into a whole number of realms from military applications to education to health care... As journalists, we need to understand how power and money moves.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke exposes the power dynamics behind AI through investigative examples: the opacity of OpenAI's training data, Brad Parscale's AI-powered campaign tools for exporting conservative victories worldwide, and the global south labor force (Kenya, Venezuela) that feeds AI systems, framing journalism as essential accountability infrastructure.

theme_codings.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and Audience Experience

best_quote.text: We're really thinking about what kinds of very personal data have we as a society given over? And how are these models now using that to predict and surveil?
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Product innovation is not a focus; Burke's concern is investigative rather than product-oriented, though she mentions AI content analysis of election messaging as a potential augmentation tool.

theme_codings.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and the Market for AI Journalism

best_quote.text: Who's benefiting and making money as a result of AI's growth.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Economic concerns are framed investigatively -- following the money behind AI growth -- rather than through the lens of newsroom business sustainability.

theme_codings.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Governance of AI

best_quote.text: AI systems are really unregulated by and large around the world. And so there's an opportunity to have real-world impact with journalism that deeply and in a nuanced fashion helps the public understand how these tools work, don't work and who they're impacting.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke positions journalism as a de facto regulator in the absence of adequate AI governance, citing AP's child welfare algorithm reporting that prompted a DOJ civil rights investigation as evidence of the reporting-prompts-regulation pipeline, while warning that existential risk narratives distract from real-world harms like voter misinformation.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Production

best_quote.text: There's some other approaches that we're considering looking at content analysis of huge amounts of data, particularly in this election cycle.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: The prototype pipeline is not a focus; Burke mentions exploring AI content analysis tools but does not discuss the mechanics of building or deploying them.

theme_codings.theme_name: Data, Privacy, and the Boundaries of Responsible AI Use

best_quote.text: We live in an age where it's so easy to clone a person's voice or face, as we've seen in the discussion about Scarlett Johansson and OpenAI's talking chatbot, that some real concerns about privacy and ownership of one's likeness are important to keep at top of mind.
best_quote.speaker: Garance Burke

theme_codings.summary: Burke raises privacy through the lens of investigative concern: personal data used for AI training, the ease of cloning voices and faces (Scarlett Johansson/OpenAI controversy), government surveillance of marginalized populations post-COVID, and the child welfare algorithm's use of sensitive family data like maternal smoking history.
